name: Preparation
description: Research the cultural context, tradition, and community of the knowledge holder before the interview. For Inuit interviews, review IQ principles and relevant seasonal/ecological context. For First Nations interviews, review nation-specific protocols and history. For Appalachian interviews, review regional geography and community history. Prepare open-ended questions that follow the knowledge holder's own framework rather than imposing an external taxonomy.

name: Relationship Building (Tunnganarniq)
description: Invest time in building genuine relationship before beginning formal documentation. The Inuit IQ principle Tunnganarniq (fostering good spirit, being open and welcoming) guides this practice: arrive with openness, not with an extraction agenda. For Inuit and First Nations elders, multiple visits before any recording may be appropriate. Relationship is not a precondition for extraction — it is the medium through which knowledge is legitimately shared.

name: Consent
description: Obtain documented consent before any recording. Consent must cover: what will be recorded, how it will be stored, who will have access, how it may be used, and whether the knowledge holder retains the right to withdraw. For First Nations knowledge keepers, OCAP-compliant community consent may be required in addition to individual consent. For Inuit elders, NISR (National Inuit Strategy on Research) compliance governs digitally shareable content. Written consent forms should be available in the knowledge holder's language.

name: Equipment Setup
description: Prepare recording equipment before the interview begins. Test audio levels, battery charge, and storage capacity. Position microphones to capture the knowledge holder clearly without placing technology as a barrier between interviewer and subject. For in-home or on-land interviews, minimize equipment footprint. Always have a backup recording method. Explain the equipment to the knowledge holder before beginning so there are no surprises.

name: Opening
description: Begin the interview with social time, not immediately with questions. Allow the knowledge holder to set the tone and pace. Introduce yourself and your purpose in their terms, not in academic or institutional language. The Foxfire student-interviewer method emphasizes that the elder should feel like they are sharing their expertise with a genuinely curious learner, not providing data to an institution. For Inuit and First Nations interviews, the opening may involve specific cultural protocols — follow the knowledge holder's lead.

name: Active Listening (Pilimmaksarniq)
description: Listen fully without interrupting to redirect the narrative toward your prepared questions. The IQ principle Pilimmaksarniq teaches that genuine learning requires patience, observation, and following the mentor's natural progression. The most important knowledge is often shared in the pauses, the digressions, and the stories that seem off-topic. Resist the urge to fill silences — silence often indicates that the knowledge holder is accessing deep memory or preparing to share something significant.

name: Follow-up Questions
description: Use follow-up questions to deepen understanding of what the knowledge holder has already shared rather than to introduce new topics. Good follow-ups begin with what the knowledge holder said: 'You mentioned [specific detail] — can you tell me more about that?' Avoid leading questions that impose your interpretation. For technical heritage skills, follow-up questions should clarify materials, seasonal timing, and the community role of the skill.

name: Closing
description: Close the interview with gratitude and clarity about next steps. Confirm with the knowledge holder what will happen to the recording and when they can expect to review any transcription or publication derived from it. Leave the door open for follow-up contact. For Inuit and First Nations knowledge keepers, the OCAP principle of Access means the knowledge holder has the right to review and correct how their knowledge is represented before any publication.

name: Transcription (Including Inuktitut Syllabics)
description: Transcribe recordings accurately, preserving the knowledge holder's own language, phrasing, and cadence. Do not paraphrase or 'clean up' speech patterns — dialect and idiom are part of the cultural record. For Inuktitut content, transcription must preserve syllabic characters (ᐃᓄᒃᑎᑐᑦ) — use Unicode syllabics block (U+1400 to U+167F) and do not substitute romanization without the knowledge holder's explicit consent. For Anishinaabemowin and other First Nations languages, follow the orthography system preferred by the knowledge holder's community.

name: Review (OCAP Access Principle)
description: Return the transcription or draft publication to the knowledge holder for review before any distribution. The OCAP principle of Access — that First Nations people must have access to information about their own communities and control over who else can access it — makes this review stage non-optional for First Nations and Inuit content. For Appalachian documentation, review by the knowledge holder is best practice. Allow the knowledge holder to correct errors, clarify context, or withdraw consent for specific sections.

name: Archiving
description: Store recordings, transcriptions, and consent documentation in a secure system with clearly documented access controls. For First Nations and Inuit content, archiving decisions should follow OCAP Possession principles: the community retains ownership of the knowledge even when the interviewer holds physical copies. Document provenance: who was interviewed, when, where, under what consent agreement, and what access restrictions apply. Never store community-restricted content (Level 3) or sacred content (Level 4) in shared or cloud systems without explicit community authorization.

name: Indigenous Protocols Acknowledgment
description: Acknowledge and follow nation-specific protocols throughout the entire oral history process. Protocols vary significantly: Haudenosaunee interviews may require opening with a Thanksgiving Address; Inuit elders may prefer on-the-land interviews integrated with active practice (consistent with IQ-Pilimmaksarniq); Anishinaabe knowledge keepers may require tobacco offerings as a request for knowledge-sharing. When in doubt, ask directly what protocols the knowledge holder expects. Do not assume one nation's protocols apply to another — pan-Indigenous assumptions cause harm.
